question: What can I do with the deploy planner?
expectedAnswer: Browse a searchable palette of 81 Azure service types in 6 categories, place them on a canvas of subscription/domain/service containers, configure SKUs and runtimes with constrained controls, draw dependency arrows, validate the plan, save it to Cosmos, estimate cost, and export a deployable .bicepparam or a standalone .bicep template.

question: Does the deploy planner run the deployment itself?
expectedAnswer: No — that is an honest gate. The planner exports the deployable file; you apply it with az deployment sub create or the deploy-fiab workflow, as surfaced in the planner's info bar.

question: What is the difference between single-sub and multi-sub deploymentMode?
expectedAnswer: single-sub puts the Admin Plane plus exactly one DLZ in the same subscription (the default for a one-domain plan). multi-sub puts the Admin Plane here and one DLZ per domain across separate subscriptions; the emitter writes a commented dlzSubscriptionIds TODO because the real sub GUIDs are an operator input.

question: What happens when deploymentMode is left unset on a subscription in the planner?
expectedAnswer: The emitter derives it from the domain count: more than one domain means multi-sub, since single-sub supports at most one DLZ. The control itself is a constrained Dropdown bound to the two @allowed values.

question: How do I add a new Data Landing Zone to an existing Loom deployment?
expectedAnswer: DLZ-attach re-runs main.bicep with an expanded dlzSubscriptionIds / dlzDomainNames; because the Admin Plane already exists, only the spoke RBAC and spoke peering for the new DLZ are added. First-run vs DLZ-attach is a Setup-Wizard/Console concept layered on the same template.

question: How are the planner's SKU and config controls constrained?
expectedAnswer: Every control is constrained, never freeform JSON: selects are Fluent Dropdowns bound to the exact @allowed list on the backing bicep module param, numbers are SpinButtons bounded by @minValue/@maxValue, and coerceConfigValue is the shared gate used by both UI and server so an out-of-range value can never reach Cosmos or the export.

question: What do dependency arrows between services become in the exported bicep template?
expectedAnswer: In the standalone .bicep export every module-backed selected service becomes a real module, and dependency arrows become module dependsOn entries, with per-resource config threaded through.

question: How does the planner's cost estimate work?
expectedAnswer: Estimate cost prices the selected subscription against the public Azure Retail Prices API using a representative SKU per service, via the /api/admin/deploy-plan/cost-estimate route.

question: Where are deploy plans saved?
expectedAnswer: Save plan persists the design to Cosmos as deploy-plan:<tenantId> in tenant-settings, via a PUT to /api/admin/deploy-plan.

question: What does the planner's Validate button check?
expectedAnswer: Validate flags dangling dependencies as errors, plus plan-only, empty, and cross-subscription warnings — the planner's equivalent of the portal's Review + create.

question: Which per-resource configuration params does the exported .bicepparam carry?
expectedAnswer: Redis SKU, App Service plan SKU + Linux runtime, Functions worker runtime + version, PostgreSQL and MySQL version + storage GB, Service Bus namespace SKU, Azure Firewall tier, and Stream Analytics streaming units — each mirroring the module decorator and forwarded by main.bicep. Interdependent knobs like Redis family/capacity are derived from the SKU so every export compiles.

question: How does the planner handle sovereign-cloud boundaries?
expectedAnswer: Each subscription carries a boundary — Commercial, GCC, GCC-High, or IL5 — and region selection is boundary-aware via per-boundary default regions.

question: Why is a fresh install a two-phase deploy (infra first, then images)?
expectedAnswer: Phase one provisions the infrastructure including an empty ACR; the Container Apps cannot start until images exist, so phase two runs the app-deploy workflow that builds and pushes every app image into the ACR and brings the Container Apps up on them. The post-deploy bootstrap then wires sign-in and data-plane grants.

question: Why can the planner emit a Redis SKU but not a Redis family/capacity pair?
expectedAnswer: Family and capacity must match the SKU, so they are derived in main.bicep from the chosen SKU — the planner never emits an invalid pairing, and every exported combination compiles and deploys.

question: Do core or plan-only services in the planner expose config knobs?
expectedAnswer: No — core (bicepFlag null) and plan-only services expose no config knobs, because a knob whose value is not honored by the deployable template would be a fake per the no-vaporware rule. Drift guards fail if a config field's bicepParam is missing from main.bicep.
